name: Buster Smith
abstract: Henry Franklin "Buster" Smith (August 24, 1904 – August 10, 1991), also known as Professor Smith, was an American jazz alto saxophonist and mentor to Charlie Parker. Smith was instrumental in instituting the Texas Sax Sound with Count Basie and Lester Young in the 1930s.

about_quotes.speaker: Buck Clayton
about_quotes.evidence: Buster Smith, yes we call him Prof... Buster Smith was a pretty good musician? Right. Yes, he was. But more of a lead than a soloist? Well, I think so. In fact, he had gone when I got there... But when Prof left, Buster, there was nobody to take it.

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: I went to Nesuhi Ertegun... I knew he would be very sympathetic to an idea of recording Buster Smith and his orchestra for Atlantic Records... We recorded that day.
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Durham
about_quotes.evidence: And that's how I met Buster Smith and I started playing with him. We played dances with some of the little groups, even with Buster's little band.

about_quotes.speaker: Samuel Lovett
about_quotes.evidence: I started playing with Buster Smith at a place called Lucille's Paradise

about_quotes.speaker: Sammy Price
about_quotes.evidence: And then you'd have Dan Miner and Budd Johnson and Keg Johnson, Booker Pittman, Count Basie, Buster Moten, Buster Smith, Hot Lips Page, Walter Page, all of the musicians that...and Ben Webster...all of the musicians that you had known over the years... WERE in Kansas City.

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Barefield
about_quotes.evidence: I knew Walter Paige before because when I was in Sioux City, Iowa, stationed there with Grant Moore, the Blue Devils were stationed there, too. They were playing in Sioux City and they had Lester Young and Buster Smith and Theodore Ross was playing lead alto.
